body:
## Summary (enhancement)

When a **detached `--isolated docker`** session's wrapped command is degraded by
**container resource exhaustion** (OOM kill or disk-full) but the inner process
still exits `0`, `start` reports the session as a clean success (`status executed`,
`exitCode 0`) with no signal that anything went wrong. Under the remove-on-finish
default established in #140, that container's filesystem is then discarded, so the
evidence is gone.

I want to be upfront about scope: in the incident that motivated this
(downstream [link-assistant/hive-mind#1990](https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/issues/1990)),
**`start` behaved correctly** тАФ the inner `solve` process genuinely caught the
disk error, continued, and exited `0`, and `start` faithfully relayed that exit
code. The *primary* fix belongs downstream (the wrapped command must not exit `0`
when its own work was cut off) and is already implemented there. This issue is a
**defense-in-depth enhancement** request for `start`, not a regression report.

## Background / what happened

Two detached docker sessions (`konard/hive-mind-dind:2.0.23`) ran a long Rust
build inside the container. `target/` grew to ~14 GB and the container ran out of
disk mid-build:

```
rustc-LLVM ERROR: IO failure on output stream: No space left on device
error: could not compile `formal-ai` (test "unit")
Caused by:
  No space left on device (os error 28)
```

The inner command kept going, then exited `0`, so `$ --status <session>` showed:

```
  status executed
  exitCode 0
  options
    isolated docker
    isolationMode detached
    autoRemoveDockerContainer false
    keepContainerOnFail false
```

A consumer (here, a Telegram bot + downstream tracking) saw `exitCode 0` and
recorded success for a run that produced no result.

## Why the existing `-1` handling (#136) doesn't cover this

#136 hardened `enrich_detached_status` so a still-running / not-yet-visible
container is reported as `executing` rather than finished, and a genuinely stopped
container resolves its real exit code from the `Exit Code:` log footer and then
`docker inspect -f '{{.State.ExitCode}}'`. That is the right behavior for the
"container hasn't reported yet" case.

But when the container's PID 1 exits `0` after surviving a resource-exhaustion
event, both the log footer and `State.ExitCode` are `0`. There is currently no
field that says "this container hit an OOM/disk-full event during the run."

## Suggested enhancement (code)

`enrich_detached_status` already shells out to `docker inspect`. Two low-cost
additions:

1. **Surface `State.OOMKilled`.** Add it alongside the existing
   `{{.State.ExitCode}}` lookup:
   ```sh
   docker inspect -f '{{.State.OOMKilled}}' <container>
   ```
   Expose it in `--status` / `--list` (e.g. `oomKilled true`) and in the
   structured/JSON status output, so consumers can treat `exitCode 0 &&
   oomKilled true` as not-a-clean-success.

2. **Don't auto-remove the filesystem of an abnormally-terminated container.**
   Under the #140 remove-on-finish default, skip removal (and emit a hint) when
   `State.OOMKilled` is true (or the container's own exit code is non-zero),
   matching the spirit of #140's investigate-on-failure opt-out тАФ so the evidence
   survives for inspection by default when the container died abnormally.

This won't catch the *disk-full-but-process-survived* variant (where PID 1 still
exits `0` and `OOMKilled` is false) тАФ that one is only reliably detectable inside
the wrapped command тАФ but it closes the OOM-kill blind spot generically for every
consumer of `$ --isolated docker`.

## Reproducible example

```sh
# A wrapped command that exhausts the container's memory but exits 0 anyway:
$ --isolated docker --detached --memory 256m -- \
  sh -c 'head -c 2G /dev/zero | tail -c 1 >/dev/null 2>&1 || true; exit 0'

# Then inspect the session тАФ exitCode is 0 with no resource-exhaustion signal:
$ --status <session-id>
#   status executed
#   exitCode 0          <-- no indication the container was OOM-killed mid-run
```

For a disk-full reproduction, run any build that writes more than the container's
free disk to a tmpfs/overlay and `|| true; exit 0` at the end.

## Downstream workaround (already shipped)

In hive-mind we no longer trust the wrapped tool's exit code alone. Each AI tool's
machine-readable stream is now required to emit its **terminal completion event**
before a run is called a success:

- codex: paired `turn.started` / `turn.completed` (or `turn.failed`) тАФ an exit-0
  run with `turn.started` but no `turn.completed` is registered as failure.
- gemini / qwen: the terminal `result` event (Claude Agent SDK schema).
- A failed run preserves the AI session for a context-preserving retry and, under
  docker isolation, returns a non-zero exit so the container filesystem is kept.

Full analysis and raw logs:
https://github.com/link-assistant/hive-mind/tree/main/docs/case-studies/issue-1990

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `--status` / `--list` (text + structured output) expose a container
      resource-exhaustion signal (at minimum `State.OOMKilled`).
- [ ] Under the #140 remove-on-finish default, an abnormally-terminated container's
      filesystem is preserved by default (with a hint), consistent with #140's
      investigate-on-failure opt-out.
- [ ] Reproducible example and downstream workaround documented (above).
title: Detached docker: surface container resource-exhaustion (OOM/disk) so a degraded exit-0 run isn't reported as clean success
